nanoLlama
01 02 03 0405 0607080910 1112131415
CPU · Pure PyTorch
Lesson 03 of 15

Dataset: Tokenization
& Vocabulary

⏱ ~50 min 📦 Data Pipeline 💻 CPU only

Neural networks eat numbers — not words. Tokenization is the bridge between raw text and tensors. We'll build a byte-level tokenizer from scratch, create our training dataset from a tiny text file, and build the PyTorch DataLoader pipeline that will feed our nanoLlama.

What is Tokenization?

Tokenization splits text into tokens — the atomic units your model reads. Each token gets a unique integer ID. The model never sees the actual characters, only integers. After processing, those integers get projected back into text.

Example: "Hello world!" → tokens → integer IDs
Hello
72
·
32
world
119
!
33
→ [72, 32, 119, 33] — a list of integers your model can process

For our nanoLlama, we'll use byte-level tokenization: each byte of the UTF-8 encoded text becomes one token. This gives us a vocabulary of exactly 256 tokens (0–255) with zero preprocessing. No special tokens, no BPE merges — just bytes.

Why byte-level for our project?

Real models (GPT-4, Llama) use Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE) with 32K–128K tokens — more efficient for long texts. For CPU training, 256 tokens is perfect: tiny vocabulary = tiny embedding table = fast training. The concepts are identical; only vocabulary size changes.

Our Dataset: TinyShakespeare

We'll train on the classic "TinyShakespeare" dataset — ~1MB of Shakespeare's plays. It's small enough to train on CPU in minutes, varied enough to produce interesting outputs, and the same dataset Karpathy used in nanoGPT.

download_data.py — get the dataset
RUN THIS FIRST
import urllib.request
import os

# Download TinyShakespeare (~1MB, no extra libraries needed)
url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/karpathy/char-rnn/master/data/tinyshakespeare/input.txt"

if not os.path.exists("input.txt"):
    urllib.request.urlretrieve(url, "input.txt")
    print("Downloaded input.txt")

with open("input.txt", "r") as f:
    text = f.read()

print(f"Total characters: {len(text):,}")    # ~1,115,394
print(f"First 200 chars:")
print(text[:200])
# First Citizen:
# Before we proceed any further, hear me speak.
# ...

Building the Byte-Level Tokenizer

Our tokenizer needs just two functions: encode (text → integers) and decode (integers → text). Since we're using bytes, the "vocabulary" is the 256 possible byte values.

tokenizer.py — complete byte-level tokenizer
CORE CODE
import torch

class ByteTokenizer:
    """Byte-level tokenizer. Vocabulary = 256 (all possible bytes).
    No special tokens. Text → bytes → integers.
    """

    def __init__(self):
        self.vocab_size = 256
        # Vocabulary is implicit: token_id == byte_value
        # Token 65 = ASCII 'A', Token 97 = 'a', Token 32 = ' ', etc.

    def encode(self, text: str) -> list[int]:
        """Convert text string to list of integer token IDs."""
        # text.encode('utf-8') → bytes object
        # list(...) → list of integers (0-255)
        return list(text.encode('utf-8'))

    def decode(self, ids: list[int]) -> str:
        """Convert list of integer token IDs back to text."""
        # bytes(ids) → bytes object
        # .decode('utf-8', errors='replace') → handles any invalid bytes
        return bytes(ids).decode('utf-8', errors='replace')

    def encode_tensor(self, text: str) -> torch.Tensor:
        """Encode text directly to a PyTorch tensor of int64."""
        return torch.tensor(self.encode(text), dtype=torch.int64)


# ── Test it ──────────────────────────────────────────────────
tokenizer = ByteTokenizer()

text = "Hello, World!"
ids  = tokenizer.encode(text)
back = tokenizer.decode(ids)

print(f"Text:    '{text}'")
print(f"IDs:     {ids}")
# [72, 101, 108, 108, 111, 44, 32, 87, 111, 114, 108, 100, 33]
print(f"Decoded: '{back}'")     # 'Hello, World!' ✓
print(f"Vocab size: {tokenizer.vocab_size}")  # 256

# Inspect individual tokens
for char, id_ in zip(text, ids):
    print(f"  '{char}' → {id_} (0x{id_:02x})")
# 'H' → 72 (0x48)
# 'e' → 101 (0x65)
# 'l' → 108 (0x6c)  ...

Vocabulary Sample

Token IDByte (hex)CharacterCategory
320x20spaceWhitespace
65–900x41–0x5AA–ZUppercase letters
97–1220x61–0x7Aa–zLowercase letters
48–570x30–0x390–9Digits
100x0A\nNewline
128–2550x80–0xFF(multi-byte UTF-8)Non-ASCII

Building the Dataset & DataLoader

Now we need to create training examples from our tokenized text. The key insight: for a language model, every position is simultaneously an input AND a target. Given tokens [t₀, t₁, t₂, t₃], we train the model to predict t₁ from t₀, t₂ from [t₀,t₁], t₃ from [t₀,t₁,t₂], and so on.

dataset.py — sliding window dataset for language modeling
FULL PIPELINE
import torch
from torch.utils.data import Dataset, DataLoader

class TextDataset(Dataset):
    """
    Sliding window dataset for next-token prediction.

    Given a token sequence of length N, we create examples of:
      input:  tokens[i : i+seq_len]
      target: tokens[i+1 : i+seq_len+1]  ← shifted by 1

    The model learns: given these tokens, predict the next one.
    """

    def __init__(self, token_ids: torch.Tensor, seq_len: int):
        self.data    = token_ids
        self.seq_len = seq_len

    def __len__(self) -> int:
        # How many windows fit? N - seq_len (we need 1 extra for target)
        return len(self.data) - self.seq_len

    def __getitem__(self, idx: int):
        # Input: seq_len tokens starting at idx
        x = self.data[idx : idx + self.seq_len]
        # Target: same window shifted right by 1 (next token at each position)
        y = self.data[idx + 1 : idx + self.seq_len + 1]
        return x, y


# ── Build the full pipeline ───────────────────────────────────
from tokenizer import ByteTokenizer  # from previous step

# Load and tokenize text
with open("input.txt", "r") as f:
    text = f.read()

tokenizer = ByteTokenizer()
all_ids   = tokenizer.encode_tensor(text)
print(f"Total tokens: {len(all_ids):,}")   # ~1,115,394

# Split 90% train / 10% validation
n          = len(all_ids)
split      = int(n * 0.9)
train_ids  = all_ids[:split]
val_ids    = all_ids[split:]

# Our nanoLlama config (CPU-friendly)
seq_len    = 64    # context window (64 tokens)
batch_size = 16    # 16 sequences per batch

# Create Dataset objects
train_ds = TextDataset(train_ids, seq_len)
val_ds   = TextDataset(val_ids, seq_len)

print(f"Train examples: {len(train_ds):,}")  # ~1,003,854
print(f"Val examples:   {len(val_ds):,}")    # ~111,529

# Create DataLoaders (handles batching, shuffling)
train_loader = DataLoader(
    train_ds,
    batch_size=batch_size,
    shuffle=True,       # shuffle for training!
    drop_last=True,      # avoid partial batches
)
val_loader = DataLoader(val_ds, batch_size=batch_size, shuffle=False)

# ── Inspect a batch ──────────────────────────────────────────
x_batch, y_batch = next(iter(train_loader))
print(f"x_batch shape: {x_batch.shape}")   # [16, 64]
print(f"y_batch shape: {y_batch.shape}")   # [16, 64]

# Look at first sequence in batch
x0 = x_batch[0]  # shape: (64,)
y0 = y_batch[0]  # shape: (64,)

print("\nFirst sequence (input):")
print(tokenizer.decode(x0.tolist()))  # a snippet of Shakespeare
print("\nTarget (shifted by 1):")
print(tokenizer.decode(y0.tolist()))  # same snippet, offset by 1 char

# Notice: target[i] = input[i+1]. Model learns to predict the next token.
💡
Why shift by 1? At position i, the model sees tokens 0…i and must predict token i+1. We process all positions in parallel during training — that's what the "causal mask" in attention enforces. We'll build that in Lesson 05.

Understanding What We Just Built

data_inspection.py — visualize the input/target relationship
INTUITION
import torch

# Example: tiny text "ABCDE"
text   = "ABCDE"
ids    = [65, 66, 67, 68, 69]  # A=65, B=66, etc.
seq_len = 3

# With seq_len=3, we get these (input, target) pairs:
# idx=0: input=[A,B,C]  target=[B,C,D]  (predict B from A, C from AB, D from ABC)
# idx=1: input=[B,C,D]  target=[C,D,E]
# idx=2: input=[C,D,E]  target=[D,E,?]  (only 2 left, but drop_last handles this)

# During training, for ONE sequence [A,B,C] with targets [B,C,D]:
# The model is simultaneously trained on 3 tasks:
#   - given [A],    predict B
#   - given [A,B],  predict C
#   - given [A,B,C], predict D
# This is why transformers are so efficient — one forward pass = seq_len tasks!

tokens = torch.tensor(ids)
for i in range(len(ids) - seq_len):
    x = tokens[i:i+seq_len]
    y = tokens[i+1:i+seq_len+1]
    print(f"x={[chr(t) for t in x.tolist()]} → y={[chr(t) for t in y.tolist()]}")
# x=['A', 'B', 'C'] → y=['B', 'C', 'D']
# x=['B', 'C', 'D'] → y=['C', 'D', 'E']

What You Now Know

  • Tokenization converts text to integer sequences
  • Byte-level tokenization: vocabulary size = 256, zero preprocessing
  • Language modeling = next-token prediction at every position
  • DataLoader handles batching, shuffling, and iteration
  • Input and target are the same sequence shifted by 1
  • How do integers become continuous vectors? (Lesson 04 — Embeddings)
Next Lesson
Lesson 04 — Embeddings: Words as Vectors
→
